quiz.title: Follow-Up Cadence Checkpoint
quiz.description: Each attempt draws 10 questions from the curated lesson pool.

questions.question_text: According to the text, where do real estate deals actually happen?
questions.explanation: Deals close through consistent follow-up, not on the first call. The follow-up process is where real results occur.

questions.question_text: In the context of seller leads, what does 'not ready today' typically mean?
questions.explanation: A seller saying 'not ready today' usually means the timing hasn't aligned yet, not that they will never sell.

options.option_text: The seller will never sell the property
The seller is waiting for a specific price
The timing has not lined up yet or the situation is not urgent enough
The seller is currently listed with an agent

questions.question_text: On day one of the BMH follow-up cadence, what action is taken after the initial call?
questions.explanation: Logging notes immediately preserves conversation details and ensures the follow-up is properly scheduled.

options.option_text: Logging notes and scheduling the follow-up

questions.question_text: On day four of the BMH cadence, the follow-up call should reference what?
questions.explanation: Referencing the previous conversation shows the seller you remember them and builds on the existing relationship.

questions.question_text: What specific question is used during the day thirty follow-up call?
questions.explanation: This simple, low-pressure question opens the door for the seller to share any changes in their situation.

options.option_text: "Have you decided to accept our offer?"

questions.question_text: What is the primary focus of the first call with a seller?
questions.explanation: The first call focuses on discovering the seller's situation and qualifying whether there is a potential deal.

questions.question_text: What is the negative perception of calling a seller just to 'check in'?
questions.explanation: Simply 'checking in' without adding value is perceived as lazy and can damage the professional relationship.

options.option_text: It is seen as too formal
It is viewed as lazy follow-up

questions.question_text: What is the goal of an 'assumptive approach' ghost text?
questions.explanation: The assumptive approach assumes continued interest while giving the seller a graceful way to opt out if timing is wrong.

options.option_text: To pressure the seller into responding immediately
To confirm interest while giving the seller an easy out if the timing is wrong
To inform the seller that the offer is expiring

questions.question_text: What is the highest daily priority for a real estate investor according to the BMH model?
questions.explanation: The follow-up list takes priority over everything else because existing leads represent the highest conversion opportunity.

options.option_text: The follow-up list in the CRM

questions.question_text: It is acceptable to skip a follow-up if you assume the seller is not interested based on previous interactions.
questions.explanation: You do not actually know the seller's mind until told directly. Assumptions lead to missed deals.

questions.question_text: How many touches does the text suggest the person who gets the deal usually makes?
questions.explanation: Five or six contacts is typically what it takes to close a deal, outlasting most competitors who give up earlier.

questions.question_text: What is the primary purpose of asking about a seller's personal detail, like a visiting relative, in a follow-up?
questions.explanation: Remembering and referencing personal details demonstrates active listening and strengthens the relationship.

options.option_text: To gather intelligence about the property
To fill time during the conversation
To demonstrate active listening and build the relationship
To determine if the seller is emotionally vulnerable

questions.question_text: What is the psychological effect of a 'value add' ghost text mentioning neighborhood activity?
questions.explanation: Local market activity creates urgency by giving the seller new information that may affect their timing decision.

options.option_text: It makes the seller feel pressured to sell
It creates a reason for the seller to reconsider their timing based on market changes
It establishes the investor as a neighborhood expert
It triggers fear of missing out on a specific offer

questions.question_text: According to the source, follow-up should be persistent but not annoying.
questions.explanation: There is a balance between staying top-of-mind and becoming a nuisance that damages the relationship.

questions.question_text: What is the difference between a 'mass blast' and a Day 2 follow-up text?
questions.explanation: The Day 2 text is personalized to the specific lead, referencing their situation, unlike a generic mass message.

options.option_text: Mass blasts are sent via email while Day 2 texts use SMS
Mass blasts include property details while Day 2 texts do not
The Day 2 text is short and personal to the specific lead

questions.question_text: What core seller pressure involves being 'done with being a landlord'?
questions.explanation: Tired landlord syndrome describes owners who are exhausted from managing rental properties and ready to sell.

questions.question_text: What is the primary goal of the initial call's discovery phase?
questions.explanation: Discovery aims to understand the seller's motivation and situation that led them toward considering a sale.

options.option_text: To understand the situation that pushed the seller toward selling
To collect contact information for the CRM

questions.question_text: Consistently referencing previous details in follow-up calls builds trust with the seller.
questions.explanation: Remembering and referencing specific details shows the seller they are valued and not just another lead.

lessons.title: Conversation Flow Mastery
lessons.description: Keep difficult conversations moving through thought experiments, option framing, price exploration, and respectful repositioning.

content.html:
What you will learn

Keep difficult conversations moving through thought experiments, option framing, price exploration, and respectful repositioning.

  • Use thought experiments to create clarity
  • Frame available paths without false urgency
  • Explore price expectations
  • Re-engage or de-position gracefully

Learner guide

  • Offer choices only when each choice is real
  • Use curiosity to test assumptions
  • Do not trap a seller into defending a number
  • A clean de-position can preserve a future relationship

Use the current written SOP and ask your manager when a live process differs from this lesson.

content.description: Accessible learner guide for Conversation Flow Mastery

cards.front: According to Module 14, what is the recommended opening line to start a seller conversation?
cards.back: Catch me up to speed. Where are you at in the process right now?

cards.front: Which questions does the flow recommend early in the call to learn what the seller has already explored?
cards.back: How have the other investors treated you?; What did your real estate agent say?

cards.front: A seller complains that the other investors were all lowballers. What is that telling you?
cards.back: Exactly how to position yourself as different

cards.front: Module 14 teaches that you should give your number first so you control the anchor.
cards.back: False

cards.front: Which question extracts the seller's price anchor without giving up leverage?
cards.back: Do you and your family have a number you are looking for?

cards.front: Which steps make up the simple 1-2-3 of a deal conversation?
cards.back: Gather information; Test the cash lane; Open creative options

cards.front: A seller says they have a higher offer from another buyer. What should you ask?
cards.back: Is that offer cash or are there contingencies attached?

cards.front: A seller goes quiet after a good first call. Which follow-up moves does the module recommend?
cards.back: Bring a new idea or a different deal structure; Offer a solution to a logistics problem they mentioned

quiz.title: Conversation Flow Mastery Checkpoint
quiz.description: Each attempt draws 10 questions from the curated lesson pool.

questions.question_text: According to Module 14, what is the recommended opening line to start a seller conversation?
questions.explanation: The module opens with 'Catch me up to speed' because it gets sellers talking without feeling interrogated and signals you are not starting from zero.

options.option_text: Catch me up to speed. Where are you at in the process right now?
What's the lowest number you would take for the house?
If I were making a cash offer, I would probably be around X.
Do you and your family have a number you are looking for?

questions.question_text: Which questions does the flow recommend early in the call to learn what the seller has already explored?
questions.explanation: Assuming the seller has explored other options, you ask how the other investors treated them and what their agent said to pull real intel.

options.option_text: How have the other investors treated you?
What did your real estate agent say?
Why hasn't your house sold yet?

questions.question_text: A seller complains that the other investors were all lowballers. What is that telling you?
questions.explanation: A complaint like that is intel - it tells you exactly how to position yourself as different from the other buyers.

options.option_text: Exactly how to position yourself as different
The lead is burned and should be closed out
You should open above market value to stand out
The seller is bluffing about talking to other investors

questions.question_text: Module 14 teaches that you should give your number first so you control the anchor.
questions.explanation: The foundational rule is the opposite - do not give your number first; extract the seller's anchor instead.

questions.question_text: Which question extracts the seller's price anchor without giving up leverage?
questions.explanation: Asking whether the family has a number they are looking for reveals where the seller is before you show your hand.

options.option_text: Do you and your family have a number you are looking for?
How much do you owe on the mortgage?
Would you take 70 percent of market value?
What did you pay for the house when you bought it?

questions.question_text: Which steps make up the simple 1-2-3 of a deal conversation?
questions.explanation: The 1-2-3 is gather information, test the cash lane, then open creative options.

options.option_text: Present a written contract before discovery
Demand the seller's bottom line before anything else

questions.question_text: A seller says they have a higher offer from another buyer. What should you ask?
questions.explanation: Asking whether the offer is cash or has contingencies makes sure the seller understands what they are actually comparing.

options.option_text: Is that offer cash or are there contingencies attached?
Can I see their contract before I respond?
What if I match their number right now?
Why haven't you signed with them already?

questions.question_text: A seller goes quiet after a good first call. Which follow-up moves does the module recommend?
questions.explanation: Silence is not rejection - call back with something new, like a different structure or a solution to a problem they mentioned.

options.option_text: Bring a new idea or a different deal structure
Offer a solution to a logistics problem they mentioned
Treat the silence as rejection and close the lead
Re-send the same offer with a 24-hour deadline

questions.question_text: What does Module 14 call the centerpiece of every negotiation call?
questions.explanation: The thought experiment - helping the seller write the ending of the movie and reverse engineering the steps - is the centerpiece of every call.

options.option_text: Getting a signature on the first call

questions.question_text: Asking 'What is the toughest part about getting there?' is designed to surface which obstacles?
questions.explanation: Painting the end state surfaces solvable obstacles like moving help, payoff timing, and post-possession needs.

questions.question_text: The price gap stays too wide and the seller will not consider terms. How do you de-position gracefully?
questions.explanation: Saying you might not be their buyer and offering to point them to the right option earns permission without pressure and keeps the relationship.

options.option_text: I might not be your buyer, and that is okay. If I am not the right fit, I will point you to the right option.
You'll be calling me back in 90 days when this doesn't sell.
I'll agree to your price now and we can renegotiate after inspection.
Let's just split the difference so nobody walks away unhappy.

questions.question_text: How do you find the real lever behind a seller's timeline?
questions.explanation: Asking what needs to be true in the next 30 to 90 days for the deal to feel like a win reveals why the date matters.

options.option_text: Ask what needs to be true for them to feel like a win if it is solved in the next 30 to 90 days
Ask when their next mortgage payment is due
Ask how long the house has been on the market
Ask if they would close tomorrow for full price

questions.question_text: The seller still insists on a retail price. What is the pivot to terms?
questions.explanation: Offering to come up toward their price in exchange for terms, then asking permission to explain, is the prescribed pivot.

options.option_text: I can come up toward your price if you will give me terms. Do you want me to explain what I mean by terms?
I'll pay retail if you cover all of the closing costs.
Retail only happens with an agent - call me when that doesn't work out.
I can do your price now, but I'll need a reduction after inspection.

questions.question_text: Even when an offer's terms fit the seller's real-life needs, their intensity about the price point usually stays the same.
questions.explanation: When terms fit the seller's real-life needs, price intensity drops.

questions.question_text: Why does the module bring up the Craigslist car listing example?
questions.explanation: Nobody lists a car with 'make me an offer' because the first number just gets shopped - the same is true with houses.

options.option_text: To show that 'make me an offer' means the first number just gets shopped to other buyers
To show cars and houses depreciate the same way
To prove online listings only attract lowballers
To argue sellers should always list with an agent first

questions.question_text: Which of these are chips you can trade that are not actual dollars?
questions.explanation: Post-possession, moving help, and payoff timing often matter more than price and cost you nothing in dollars.

questions.question_text: What is the cash lane used for early in the conversation?
questions.explanation: The cash lane gathers truth - it tests the seller's reaction, reveals expectations and existing offers, and opens the door to terms.

options.option_text: Testing the seller's reaction to a price
Gathering the truth about expectations and existing offers
Opening the door to a terms conversation
Locking the seller into a verbal price commitment
Replacing the need for any follow-up calls

questions.question_text: Telling a seller 'I would rather give you a number I can actually perform on' accomplishes what?
questions.explanation: Being direct separates you from buyers who quote high then demand reductions, and establishes certainty as a form of value most buyers cannot offer.

options.option_text: It separates you from buyers who quote high and come back for reductions later
It establishes certainty as a form of value most buyers cannot offer
It guarantees the seller signs on the first call
It anchors the seller to a higher price

lessons.title: Closing and Deal Engineering
lessons.description: Prepare and present an offer as a clear next step while protecting accuracy, consent, and handoff quality.

content.html:
What you will learn

Prepare and present an offer as a clear next step while protecting accuracy, consent, and handoff quality.

  • Prepare the information needed for an offer
  • Present terms cleanly
  • Identify unresolved decision barriers
  • Confirm the next action without pressure

Learner guide

  • A strong close starts with accurate discovery
  • State what is known and what still requires confirmation
  • Let the seller evaluate the tradeoff
  • Document the decision and exact next step

Use the current written SOP and ask your manager when a live process differs from this lesson.

content.description: Accessible learner guide for Closing and Deal Engineering

cards.front: Why should the closing stage ideally be the easiest part of a successful sales process?
cards.back: Because the rapport, discovery, and objection handling have already been completed successfully

cards.front: The close is best described as the _____ that makes the decision official.
cards.back: Formal agreement

cards.front: How does a representative's discovery work directly support the acquisition team during the close?
cards.back: It provides the specific information and documentation the team needs to structure the offer

cards.front: Why is it important for a representative to understand how the closing process works? (Select all that apply)
cards.back: It allows them to help with follow-ups; It helps them perform better discovery for the acquisition team

cards.front: What specific benefits are typically included in a cash offer presentation? (Select all that apply)
cards.back: Closing on the seller's timeline; Handling paperwork; Requiring no repairs or fees

cards.front: What is the first step the acquisition team takes when a seller objects to the offer price?
cards.back: Acknowledging the seller's concern with empathy

cards.front: What specific question is used to identify the seller's expectations during a negotiation?
cards.back: "What number would you need to see to feel good about this?"

cards.front: What is the likely outcome if a seller feels they 'lost' during the negotiation?
cards.back: The deal will fall apart after the contract is signed

quiz.title: Closing and Deal Engineering Checkpoint
quiz.description: Each attempt draws 10 questions from the curated lesson pool.

questions.question_text: Why should the closing stage ideally be the easiest part of a successful sales process?
questions.explanation: When rapport, discovery, and objection handling are done well beforehand, the close becomes a natural conclusion rather than a hard sell.

options.option_text: Because the rapport, discovery, and objection handling have already been completed successfully
Because the seller has no other options at that point
Because the acquisition team applies heavy pressure to finalize the deal
Because the price has already been agreed upon during the first call

questions.question_text: The close is best described as the _____ that makes the decision official.
questions.explanation: The close is the formal agreement that finalizes what the seller has already decided.

questions.question_text: How does a representative's discovery work directly support the acquisition team during the close?
questions.explanation: Thorough discovery gives the acquisition team the specific details they need to structure an accurate and compelling offer.

options.option_text: It provides the specific information and documentation the team needs to structure the offer
It eliminates the need for negotiation entirely
It allows the representative to close the deal themselves
It generates the purchase agreement automatically

questions.question_text: Why is it important for a representative to understand how the closing process works? (Select all that apply)
questions.explanation: Understanding the close helps representatives contribute to follow-ups and conduct better discovery that serves the acquisition team.

options.option_text: It allows them to help with follow-ups
It helps them perform better discovery for the acquisition team
It qualifies them to negotiate deals independently
It replaces the need for an acquisition team

questions.question_text: What specific benefits are typically included in a cash offer presentation? (Select all that apply)
questions.explanation: Cash offers highlight convenience factors like flexible timelines, handled paperwork, and no costs to the seller.

questions.question_text: What is the first step the acquisition team takes when a seller objects to the offer price?
questions.explanation: Acknowledging the concern with empathy validates the seller's feelings before attempting to reframe.

options.option_text: Acknowledging the seller's concern with empathy
Explaining why the seller's expectations are unrealistic
Ending the call and following up later

questions.question_text: What specific question is used to identify the seller's expectations during a negotiation?
questions.explanation: This open-ended question lets the seller name their number, which helps the team understand the gap to close.

options.option_text: "What number would you need to see to feel good about this?"
"What is the lowest price you would accept?"
"How quickly do you need to close?"

questions.question_text: What is the likely outcome if a seller feels they 'lost' during the negotiation?
questions.explanation: Seller's remorse from feeling they lost leads to deals falling apart even after contracts are signed.

options.option_text: The deal will fall apart after the contract is signed
The seller will request a longer closing timeline
The seller will leave a negative review
The acquisition team will need to renegotiate

questions.question_text: A purchase agreement is typically prepared and sent to be signed electronically.
questions.explanation: Electronic signing streamlines the process and allows for quick turnaround after verbal agreement.

questions.question_text: Which team manages the deal once the contract has been signed?
questions.explanation: After signing, the transaction team takes over to handle all logistics through closing.

questions.question_text: What responsibilities does the transaction coordinator handle? (Select all that apply)
questions.explanation: The transaction coordinator manages all post-contract logistics to ensure a smooth closing.

questions.question_text: What is a primary root cause of 'seller's remorse'?
questions.explanation: When sellers don't feel heard, they second-guess their decision, leading to remorse and potential deal cancellation.

options.option_text: The seller feeling like they weren't heard during the sales process
The offer price being too low
The closing timeline being too long

questions.question_text: Which of the following are common title issues that can cause a deal to fall apart? (Select all that apply)
questions.explanation: Liens, boundary disputes, and unclear ownership are legal encumbrances that must be resolved before closing.

questions.question_text: According to the text, trust is more powerful than price in securing a deal.
questions.explanation: Trust-based relationships prevent sellers from leaving for marginally higher offers from competitors.

questions.question_text: A 'well-documented' lead contains thorough notes and property data necessary for making an accurate offer.
questions.explanation: Complete documentation allows the acquisition team to structure offers without needing to go back for missing information.

questions.question_text: In an offer presentation, the phrase 'How does that sound?' is used to avoid appearing _____.
questions.explanation: This soft question invites dialogue rather than creating pressure, keeping the seller comfortable.

questions.question_text: The negotiation process is described as a way to 'close the _____' between two parties.
questions.explanation: Negotiation bridges the gap between what the buyer can offer and what the seller expects.

questions.question_text: What is the final stage for a deal before it generates revenue?
questions.explanation: The closing and funds transfer, managed by the transaction team, is the last step before the deal produces revenue.

options.option_text: The closing/funds transfer managed by the transaction team
The verbal agreement from the seller
The signing of the purchase agreement

assignment.title: Follow-Up and Closing Plan
assignment.instructions: Create a 30-day follow-up plan for the probate practice situation. Include purpose, channel, message angle, stop conditions, CRM note, and next action for each touch. Finish with the conditions required for a clean offer conversation without rushing the estate process.

rubric.description: Touches are intentional, spaced, and tied to a relevant reason.
The plan honors preferences, opt-outs, and approved channels.
Separates confirmed facts from items that still require validation.

modules.title: Performance and Career
modules.description: Use scorecards, operating discipline, and coaching to improve and grow.

lessons.title: KPIs and Sales Telemetry
lessons.description: Read the funnel from left to right to locate process gaps and choose the right coaching response.

content.html:
What you will learn

Read the funnel from left to right to locate process gaps and choose the right coaching response.

  • Define the six core metrics
  • Separate activity from productive progress
  • Diagnose where a funnel breaks
  • Use metrics for coaching rather than punishment

Learner guide

  • Metrics show where to investigate. They do not explain everything by themselves
  • Use the current role-and-market scorecard. Training does not set one universal numeric target
  • Check conversion between adjacent stages
  • Pair numbers with notes and call review before deciding on a fix

Use the current written SOP and ask your manager when a live process differs from this lesson.

content.description: Accessible learner guide for KPIs and Sales Telemetry

cards.front: According to the text, what is the only thing that actually closes deals in business?
cards.back: Numbers

cards.front: What analogy is used to describe how KPIs inform a manager about progress?
cards.back: A car's dashboard

cards.front: KPIs are the tool used to separate being busy from being _____.
cards.back: Productive

cards.front: The six pipeline metrics are tracked from left to right.
cards.back: True

cards.front: Why is dial count considered a unique metric compared to others like connection rate?
cards.back: It is the only metric the caller has one hundred percent control over

cards.front: Dial count is tracked as a leading indicator of _____, rather than as the ultimate goal.
cards.back: Effort

cards.front: What should happen when connection rate falls below the normal range for the current role and market?
cards.back: Flag it to the manager and check number reputation, list quality, and call timing

cards.front: What is a common technical cause for a sudden drop in connection rates?
cards.back: The phone number getting marked as spam

quiz.title: KPIs and Sales Telemetry Checkpoint
quiz.description: Each attempt draws 10 questions from the curated lesson pool.

questions.question_text: According to the text, what is the only thing that actually closes deals in business?
questions.explanation: Numbers and data-driven decisions are what ultimately drive deal closures, not just effort or activity.

questions.question_text: What analogy is used to describe how KPIs inform a manager about progress?
questions.explanation: Just as a car's dashboard shows speed, fuel, and engine status at a glance, KPIs show performance metrics.

questions.question_text: KPIs are the tool used to separate being busy from being _____.
questions.explanation: Activity without results is just busyness; KPIs reveal whether effort is translating into productive outcomes.

questions.question_text: The six pipeline metrics are tracked from left to right.
questions.explanation: Metrics flow left to right through the pipeline, from initial dials through to signed contracts.

questions.question_text: Why is dial count considered a unique metric compared to others like connection rate?
questions.explanation: Unlike connection rate or quality conversations, how many times you dial is entirely within your control.

options.option_text: It is the only metric the caller has one hundred percent control over
It is the most difficult metric to achieve
It requires no skill to track

questions.question_text: Dial count is tracked as a leading indicator of _____, rather than as the ultimate goal.
questions.explanation: Dial count measures effort and activity levels, which are precursors to downstream results.

questions.question_text: What should happen when connection rate falls below the normal range for the current role and market?
questions.explanation: The current KPI lesson treats a connection-rate change as a diagnostic signal: check number reputation, list quality, and call timing with the manager.

options.option_text: Flag it to the manager and check number reputation, list quality, and call timing
Treat the change as proof that the representative is not working
Speed-dial until the connection rate returns to normal
Ignore it because connection rate cannot reveal an operating issue

questions.question_text: What is a common technical cause for a sudden drop in connection rates?
questions.explanation: Phone numbers flagged as spam are blocked or screened by carriers, dramatically reducing connections.

options.option_text: The phone number getting marked as spam
A software update on the dialer
The time of day the calls are made

questions.question_text: What does the 'quality conversations' metric specifically filter out? (Select all that apply)
questions.explanation: Quality conversations only count real prospects, excluding wrong numbers, removal requests, and completely uninterested parties.

options.option_text: People with zero interest in selling

questions.question_text: A caller with high quality conversations but low process calls is likely failing at what skill?
questions.explanation: If prospects are engaged but not progressing, the rep likely struggles with handling objections or conducting deep discovery.

questions.question_text: Which metric represents the final stage of the funnel where offers are accepted?
questions.explanation: Contracts signed is the bottom of the funnel, representing successfully closed deals.

questions.question_text: Why are the six KPI metrics read from left to right?
questions.explanation: Reading the funnel from left to right helps the team locate the actual gap and respond with the relevant operational check or coaching.

options.option_text: To pinpoint the stage where the process is breaking and choose the right response
To assign one universal activity target to every role and market
To rank representatives without reviewing the underlying work
To replace coaching and call review with a single number

questions.question_text: If connections are high but quality conversations are few, what is likely the issue?
questions.explanation: Reaching people who aren't the right audience or failing to engage them suggests targeting or script problems.

options.option_text: The audience is wrong or the opening script needs work
The phone number is flagged as spam
The rep is not dialing enough
The acquisition team is not making offers

questions.question_text: If a rep has high offers but few signed contracts, where is the breakdown occurring?
questions.explanation: When offers are being made but not accepted, the issue lies in negotiation effectiveness or pricing accuracy.

questions.question_text: When a metric is off, what is the primary question managers and reps should ask?
questions.explanation: This diagnostic question focuses on identifying and solving the specific breakdown rather than assigning blame.

options.option_text: Where is the gap and what do we do about it?
Who is responsible for this failure?
Is this person the right fit for the role?

questions.question_text: What is a 'leading indicator' in the context of KPIs?
questions.explanation: Leading indicators like dial count measure current effort that predicts future results, unlike lagging indicators that measure past outcomes.

options.option_text: A metric like dial count that tracks the effort required to reach future results
The most important metric in the pipeline
A metric that only managers can track
The final metric that determines commission payouts

questions.question_text: Some reps have low dial counts despite being busy because they may be spending too long on individual calls.
questions.explanation: Spending excessive time on single calls reduces overall dial volume even when the rep is actively working.

questions.question_text: The fundamental philosophy behind tracking the six metrics is to find gaps and close them rather than to micromanage.
questions.explanation: Metrics serve as diagnostic tools for improvement, not surveillance tools for control.

lessons.title: Compensation Engine
lessons.description: Understand that compensation depends on the value a role owns and that the current written plan is always the source of truth.

content.html:
What you will learn

Understand that compensation depends on the value a role owns and that the current written plan is always the source of truth.

  • Identify the current compensation source of truth
  • Connect role outcomes to the role scorecard
  • Know where to take compensation questions
  • Avoid relying on another person's plan or an old example

Learner guide

  • There is no universal formula for every role
  • Use your current offer letter, written plan, or role sheet
  • Ask your manager when any part of the plan is unclear
  • Do not treat training examples or conversations as a compensation promise

Use the current written SOP and ask your manager when a live process differs from this lesson.

content.description: Accessible learner guide for Compensation Engine

cards.front: Why does the lesson avoid one universal compensation formula?

cards.back: What your role owns and how success is measured

cards.front: A role scorecard can change as the business changes.
cards.back: True

quiz.title: Compensation Engine Checkpoint
quiz.description: Each attempt draws 10 questions from the curated lesson pool.

questions.question_text: What is the source of truth for your current compensation plan?
questions.explanation: Your current offer letter, compensation plan, or role-specific sheet controls.

questions.question_text: Why does the lesson avoid one universal compensation formula?
questions.explanation: Compensation is designed around the value and outcomes assigned to each role.

options.option_text: The company does not measure performance

questions.question_text: Which documents may contain the current written plan?
questions.explanation: Use the current written documents issued for your role.

questions.question_text: If your written plan and a training example appear different, which one applies?
questions.explanation: Training explains the engine. The current written plan defines what applies to you.

questions.question_text: Where should you take a question about what applies to your role?
questions.explanation: Your manager and your current written plan are the two approved sources.

lessons.title: Operator Playbook and Daily Mission Control
lessons.description: Run a disciplined workday, keep the pipeline current, and communicate decisions and blockers through the team's operating systems.

content.html:
What you will learn

Run a disciplined workday, keep the pipeline current, and communicate decisions and blockers through the team's operating systems.

  • Prioritize daily lead work
  • Maintain notes, stages, and next actions
  • Use team communication channels professionally
  • Close the day with a clean pipeline and clear plan

Learner guide

  • Begin with scheduled and high-priority follow-ups
  • Log notes immediately after meaningful activity
  • Ask rather than guess when a situation is unfamiliar
  • A worked day reflects complete controllable actions, not a guaranteed outcome

Use the current written SOP and ask your manager when a live process differs from this lesson.

questions.explanation: The pipeline review ends with every active lead carrying a next action and a specific date.

lessons.title: Career Growth Path
lessons.description: Build capability in your current role through deliberate practice, useful feedback, coachability, and reliable execution.

content.html:
What you will learn

Build capability in your current role through deliberate practice, useful feedback, coachability, and reliable execution.

  • Use a practice and feedback loop
  • Respond to coaching without defensiveness
  • Connect capability to current role expectations
  • Confirm any increase in ownership with your manager and current written role plan

Learner guide

  • Practice, apply feedback, and review the result
  • Capability means using knowledge and skills reliably in current work
  • Your manager and current written role plan define your responsibilities and expectations
  • Take on increased ownership only after the change is documented in your current written role plan

Use the current written SOP and ask your manager when a live process differs from this lesson.

assignment.title: Mission Control and Growth Capstone
assignment.instructions: Build a one-day operating plan with priorities, checkpoints, metrics, pipeline hygiene, team communication, and end-of-day review. Add a short reflection on one skill to improve, how you will measure it, and how you will use coaching. Finish with two practice debriefs: how you preserved authority and neutrality in the family-dynamics situation, and what you would carry forward from the full-cycle seller conversation.

rubric.description: The day protects follow-ups, documentation, communication, breaks, and review.
Chooses metrics that reveal a specific process gap without inventing targets.
Names a concrete practice and feedback loop tied to the current role.
Uses specific evidence from both required Section 6 role plays to identify a safe behavior to repeat or improve.
